content.text: A webhook is an HTTP callback — a user-defined URL that a service calls whenever a specified event occurs. Rather than the consumer continuously polling for changes, the provider pushes notifications by making outbound HTTP POST requests to the registered endpoint. Webhooks power real-time integrations between payment processors, version control systems, CI/CD pipelines, communication platforms, and AI orchestration layers.

A webhook is a pattern, not a protocol. It consists of three components: a registration step (the consumer tells the provider where to send events), an event trigger (the provider detects a qualifying event in its system), and a delivery step (the provider sends an HTTP POST request to the registered URL with a JSON payload describing the event).

The consumer's webhook endpoint receives the request and returns an HTTP 200 status within a provider-defined timeout (typically 5–30 seconds). Any non-2xx response — or a timeout — causes the provider to retry the delivery according to its retry policy. This at-least-once delivery semantic means consumers must implement idempotency: processing the same event twice must produce the same result as processing it once.

Modern webhook implementations include a cryptographic signature on every delivery — typically an HMAC-SHA256 over the raw request body using a shared secret. Consumers must verify this signature before processing any payload, preventing webhook spoofing by malicious actors who may discover the endpoint URL.

Polling — repeatedly querying an API for changes — wastes bandwidth, increases latency, and strains provider infrastructure. A service checking for new payments every 5 seconds makes 17,280 API calls per day, most of which return nothing. Webhooks eliminate this waste: events arrive within seconds of occurring, with zero idle API calls.

In AI and agentic systems, webhooks enable asynchronous completion of long-running tasks. An agent that submits a document for OCR processing or triggers a remote code execution task cannot block an HTTP connection for minutes. The pattern is: submit the task, receive an immediate acknowledgement with a job ID, then receive a webhook when the job completes.

Webhooks are the backbone of event-driven architectures. GitHub Actions, Stripe payment flows, Twilio message status updates, Slack command responses, and most PaaS deployment pipelines all deliver their integration surface through webhooks.
Events arrive within seconds of occurring, not on the next polling interval.
Provider infrastructure does not serve thousands of empty polling responses per consumer per day.
The provider does not need to know anything about the consumer's internal system — it simply calls a URL.
Long-running jobs (OCR, video transcoding, LLM batch inference) can notify consumers upon completion without maintaining an open HTTP connection.
A single event can be delivered to multiple registered webhook URLs, enabling multiple systems to react independently.
Every webhook delivery can be logged with payload, headers, response code, and timestamp — a complete audit trail of inter-system events.
Providers retry failed deliveries, so consumers must handle duplicate events — idempotency is non-optional.
Network conditions can cause events to arrive out of order; consumers must handle or ignore out-of-order events.
The provider must be able to reach the webhook endpoint via the public internet; local development requires tunnels (ngrok, Cloudflare Tunnel).
A spike in events generates a corresponding spike in inbound HTTP requests; the consumer must handle bursts or queue and process asynchronously.
Tight coupling to provider retry policies:
If the consumer is temporarily unavailable and the provider exhausts its retries, events are lost unless a dead-letter mechanism exists.

The consumer registers a webhook endpoint URL with the provider, specifying which event types to subscribe to. Registration is typically done via the provider's dashboard or API. The provider returns a shared secret, which both sides use for HMAC signature generation and verification.

When a qualifying event occurs in the provider's system, it creates a delivery record and sends an HTTP POST to all registered webhook URLs for that event type. The request body is a JSON document describing the event. Standard headers include the event type, a unique delivery ID, a timestamp, and the HMAC signature.
The provider computes a signature over the raw request body using the shared secret:

Stripe:    Stripe-Signature: t=1614000000,v1=<hmac_sha256>
GitHub:    X-Hub-Signature-256: sha256=<hmac_sha256>
Generic:   X-Webhook-Signature: sha256=<hmac_sha256>

The consumer recomputes the HMAC over the received body with the same secret and compares it to the received header using a timing-safe comparison. Matching signatures confirm the payload is authentic and unmodified.

The consumer must return 2xx within the provider's timeout window (often 10–30 seconds). Any non-2xx response or timeout causes the provider to schedule a retry with exponential backoff. Most providers retry 3–10 times over minutes to hours before moving the delivery to a dead-letter queue or abandoning it.

attrs.alt: Webhook flow: registration, event delivery with HMAC, async queue, retry policy
Webhook flow: registration, event delivery, HMAC verification, and idempotent handling

content.text: Events are infrequent, irregular, or unpredictable — polling is wasteful when events arrive at low and variable rates.
The consumer needs to react to external state changes in near-real-time without maintaining an open connection.
The system involves third-party service integrations (payment providers, SCM platforms, communication APIs).
Long-running async tasks (batch inference, file processing) must notify consumers upon completion.
Building event-driven microservice architectures where services communicate through events rather than direct API calls.
The consumer cannot be reached over the public internet (embedded devices, private networks without a tunnel).
Strict exactly-once semantics are required — webhooks provide at-least-once at best; use transactional messaging systems (Kafka, SQS FIFO) instead.
The event volume is extremely high and every event must be processed — a dedicated message queue is more appropriate than direct HTTP callbacks.

import hashlib, hmac, json, os
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException
import asyncio

app = FastAPI()
WEBHOOK_SECRET = os.environ["WEBHOOK_SECRET"]

def verify_signature(payload: bytes, signature_header: str, secret: str) -> bool:
    """Verify HMAC-SHA256 signature in constant time."""
    expected = hmac.new(
        secret.encode(), payload, hashlib.sha256
    ).hexdigest()
    # Strip "sha256=" prefix if present
    received = signature_header.removeprefix("sha256=")
    return hmac.compare_digest(expected, received)

@app.post("/webhooks/events")
async def receive_webhook(request: Request):
    body = await request.body()
    sig = request.headers.get("X-Webhook-Signature", "")

    if not verify_signature(body, sig, WEBHOOK_SECRET):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid signature")

    event = json.loads(body)
    event_type = event.get("type")
    event_id = event.get("id")

    # Return 200 IMMEDIATELY — never process inline
    # Enqueue for async processing
    asyncio.create_task(process_event_async(event_type, event_id, event))
    return {"status": "accepted", "event_id": event_id}

async def process_event_async(event_type: str, event_id: str, payload: dict):
    """Process the event outside the HTTP request lifecycle."""
    # Deduplication, business logic, downstream calls go here
    print(f"Processing {event_type} event {event_id}")

import redis
from datetime import timedelta

r = redis.Redis()
DEDUP_TTL = timedelta(hours=24)

async def process_event_async(event_type: str, event_id: str, payload: dict):
    """Idempotent event processor using Redis deduplication."""
    key = f"webhook:processed:{event_id}"
    # SETNX returns False if key already exists (event already processed)
    if not r.set(key, "1", ex=DEDUP_TTL, nx=True):
        print(f"Duplicate event {event_id} — skipping")
        return
    try:
        await handle_event(event_type, payload)
    except Exception as e:
        # On failure, delete the dedup key so the event can be reprocessed
        r.delete(key)
        raise

async def handle_event(event_type: str, payload: dict):
    if event_type == "payment.succeeded":
        await fulfil_order(payload["data"]["object"])
    elif event_type == "subscription.cancelled":
        await deactivate_account(payload["data"]["object"])
    # ... more handlers
Example 1 — GitHub webhook for CI/CD trigger

import hashlib, hmac, json, os, subprocess
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException

app = FastAPI()
GITHUB_SECRET = os.environ["GITHUB_WEBHOOK_SECRET"]

def verify_github_signature(body: bytes, signature: str) -> bool:
    """GitHub uses sha256= prefix on the signature."""
    mac = hmac.new(GITHUB_SECRET.encode(), body, hashlib.sha256)
    expected = f"sha256={mac.hexdigest()}"
    return hmac.compare_digest(expected, signature)

@app.post("/webhooks/github")
async def github_webhook(request: Request):
    body = await request.body()
    sig = request.headers.get("X-Hub-Signature-256", "")
    event_type = request.headers.get("X-GitHub-Event", "")
    delivery_id = request.headers.get("X-GitHub-Delivery", "")

    if not verify_github_signature(body, sig):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Bad signature")

    payload = json.loads(body)
    print(f"Event: {event_type}, Delivery: {delivery_id}")

    if event_type == "push" and payload.get("ref") == "refs/heads/main":
        # Trigger deployment asynchronously
        subprocess.Popen(["./scripts/deploy.sh", payload["after"]])

    elif event_type == "pull_request" and payload.get("action") == "opened":
        pr_number = payload["pull_request"]["number"]
        print(f"New PR #{pr_number}: {payload['pull_request']['title']}")

    return {"status": "ok", "delivery_id": delivery_id}
Example 2 — OpenAI Assistants API async run completion webhook

import json, os, hashlib, hmac
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

app = FastAPI()
client = AsyncOpenAI()
OPENAI_WEBHOOK_SECRET = os.environ.get("OPENAI_WEBHOOK_SECRET", "")

@app.post("/webhooks/openai")
async def openai_webhook(request: Request):
    body = await request.body()
    sig = request.headers.get("openai-signature", "")

    if OPENAI_WEBHOOK_SECRET:
        mac = hmac.new(OPENAI_WEBHOOK_SECRET.encode(), body, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
        if not hmac.compare_digest(mac, sig):
            raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid signature")

    event = json.loads(body)
    event_type = event.get("type")

    if event_type == "thread.run.completed":
        run_id = event["data"]["id"]
        thread_id = event["data"]["thread_id"]
        # Retrieve the assistant's response
        messages = await client.beta.threads.messages.list(thread_id=thread_id)
        latest = messages.data[0]
        content = latest.content[0].text.value if latest.content else ""
        print(f"Run {run_id} completed. Response: {content[:200]}")

    elif event_type == "thread.run.failed":
        run = event["data"]
        print(f"Run {run['id']} failed: {run.get('last_error')}")

    return {"acknowledged": True}
Example 3 — Webhook forwarding for local development

# Option 1: ngrok (most popular)
ngrok http 8000
# Exposes https://abc123.ngrok.io → localhost:8000
# Use the ngrok URL as your webhook endpoint in provider's dashboard

# Option 2: Cloudflare Tunnel
cloudflared tunnel --url http://localhost:8000

# Option 3: Stripe CLI (Stripe-specific, most convenient)
stripe listen --forward-to http://localhost:8000/webhooks/stripe
# Automatically signs events with a test secret — no ngrok needed
# Press Ctrl+C to stop. Outputs logs in real time.

# Option 4: GitHub CLI + smee.io
npx smee-client --url https://smee.io/YOUR_CHANNEL --path /webhooks/github --port 8000

A SaaS platform processes customer payments via Stripe. On successful payment, it must provision the customer's subscription, send a confirmation email, update a CRM, and trigger onboarding in an internal tool. These actions are triggered by the payment_intent.succeeded webhook. The system uses an event bus pattern: the webhook receiver validates, deduplicates, and enqueues the event into a Redis Stream, from which multiple independent consumers (subscription service, email service, CRM sync) each read and process the event independently.

import stripe, json, os, redis
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException
import asyncio

app = FastAPI()
stripe.api_key = os.environ["STRIPE_SECRET_KEY"]
STRIPE_SECRET = os.environ["STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET"]
r = redis.Redis(decode_responses=True)

@app.post("/webhooks/stripe")
async def stripe_handler(request: Request):
    body = await request.body()
    sig = request.headers.get("stripe-signature")
    try:
        event = stripe.Webhook.construct_event(body, sig, STRIPE_SECRET)
    except Exception as e:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail=str(e))

    # Idempotency: skip if already seen
    event_id = event["id"]
    if r.exists(f"webhook:seen:{event_id}"):
        return {"skipped": True}
    r.setex(f"webhook:seen:{event_id}", 86400, "1")

    # Publish to Redis Stream for fan-out to multiple consumers
    r.xadd("stripe_events", {
        "event_id": event_id,
        "event_type": event["type"],
        "payload": json.dumps(event["data"]["object"]),
    })
    return {"accepted": True}

# Consumer 1: subscription provisioner (in a separate process)
async def subscription_consumer():
    last_id = "0"
    while True:
        events = r.xread({"stripe_events": last_id}, block=5000, count=10)
        for stream, messages in events:
            for msg_id, data in messages:
                if data["event_type"] == "payment_intent.succeeded":
                    await provision_subscription(json.loads(data["payload"]))
                last_id = msg_id

async def provision_subscription(pi: dict):
    customer_id = pi.get("customer")
    print(f"Provisioning subscription for customer {customer_id}")
    # Update database, send to downstream service...
Mistake 1 — Processing the webhook synchronously and timing out

# WRONG: slow processing causes provider to retry, creating duplicate work
@app.post("/webhooks/events")
async def bad_handler(request: Request):
    body = await request.body()
    event = json.loads(body)
    # This takes 30 seconds — provider timeout exceeded → retry → duplicate
    await send_email(event)          # slow
    await update_database(event)     # slow
    await call_external_api(event)   # slow
    return {"ok": True}

# RIGHT: acknowledge immediately, process in background
@app.post("/webhooks/events")
async def good_handler(request: Request):
    body = await request.body()
    event = json.loads(body)
    asyncio.create_task(process_async(event))  # fire and forget
    return {"accepted": True}  # responds in < 100ms
Mistake 2 — Not verifying the HMAC signature

# WRONG: accepting any POST request without verification
# Attacker can POST fabricated events to your endpoint
@app.post("/webhooks/events")
async def bad_webhook(request: Request):
    event = await request.json()  # trusting unauthenticated payload
    await fulfil_order(event["data"])  # dangerous

# RIGHT: always verify before processing
@app.post("/webhooks/events")
async def good_webhook(request: Request):
    body = await request.body()
    sig = request.headers.get("X-Webhook-Signature", "")
    if not verify_signature(body, sig, WEBHOOK_SECRET):
        raise HTTPException(401, "Bad signature")
    event = json.loads(body)
    asyncio.create_task(process_event_async(event))
    return {"ok": True}

Provider retry policies guarantee at-least-once delivery. If the consumer crashes after processing but before returning 200, the provider will retry and the consumer will process the event again. Without deduplication (by event ID in a persistent store), the order is fulfilled twice, the email is sent twice, and the subscription is provisioned twice.
Always verify HMAC signatures using constant-time comparison before processing any payload.
Return 200 within 5 seconds — enqueue the event and process asynchronously.
Implement idempotency using the provider's event ID stored in a persistent cache (Redis, database).
Handle out-of-order events by including event timestamps and using idempotent state transitions.
Use a dead-letter queue to capture events that exhaust all provider retries for manual review.
Log every delivery attempt with: event ID, type, delivery timestamp, response code, and processing duration.
Rotate webhook secrets periodically; support a brief overlap window during rotation to allow in-flight events.
Use Stripe CLI, ngrok, or smee.io for local development testing — never test webhooks only in production.
Monitor webhook processing lag (time from event creation to consumer processing) as a reliability metric.

A single webhook endpoint running synchronously can typically handle hundreds of requests per second given fast I/O operations. The critical constraint is the provider's timeout: if processing takes more than 5–30 seconds, the provider retries, creating exponential load during incidents. Always decouple reception from processing.

For high-volume event streams (thousands of events per second from major payment processors during peak periods), the webhook receiver should be a lightweight service whose only job is signature verification, deduplication, and enqueuing to a message bus. The actual business logic runs in separate horizontally-scaled worker pools.

Webhook deduplication stores can become bottlenecks. Redis SETNX is O(1) and sub-millisecond; a database unique constraint check adds 1–5ms. For very high throughput, use Redis with a configurable TTL (24 hours covers most provider retry windows) rather than a database for the hot deduplication path.

from svix import Svix, MessageIn

svix = Svix("your_auth_token")

# Send a webhook (as a provider)
svix.message.create(
    "app_id",
    MessageIn(
        event_type="user.signup",
        payload={"id": "user_123", "email": "user@example.com"},
    )
)

# Svix handles: delivery, retries, HMAC signing, dashboard, consumer management

Hookdeck provides a managed gateway between providers and consumers: it receives webhooks, buffers them, adds deduplication, rate-limits delivery to the consumer, and provides a replay interface for failed events. This removes the reliability burden from the consumer application.
ngrok / Cloudflare Tunnel for local development

# ngrok: immediate tunnel, no account required for basic use
ngrok http 8000

# Cloudflare Tunnel: persistent named tunnel, requires account
cloudflared tunnel create my-tunnel
cloudflared tunnel route dns my-tunnel webhook.myapp.dev
cloudflared tunnel run my-tunnel

Naive string comparison (==) is susceptible to timing attacks that leak the length and prefix of the expected HMAC. Always use hmac.compare_digest() (Python), crypto.timingSafeEqual() (Node.js), or an equivalent constant-time comparison function.

If an attacker captures a legitimate webhook delivery, they can replay it later. Stripe mitigates this by including a timestamp in the Stripe-Signature header and rejecting events older than 5 minutes. Implement timestamp validation: reject any event whose timestamp is more than N minutes in the past.

import time

def validate_timestamp(timestamp: int, tolerance_seconds: int = 300) -> bool:
    """Reject events older than tolerance_seconds to prevent replay attacks."""
    return abs(time.time() - timestamp) <= tolerance_seconds

If your application allows users to register webhook URLs that you then call (acting as a provider), validate that the registered URL does not point to internal services (169.254.0.0/16, 10.0.0.0/8, 127.0.0.1, localhost, or internal DNS names). This prevents Server-Side Request Forgery attacks where a malicious user registers an internal service URL to probe your infrastructure.

Keep webhook endpoint URLs semi-secret: do not publish them in frontend code or public repositories. Use a random suffix (e.g., /webhooks/stripe/xk9m2p7q) to reduce discoverability. This is defence in depth — not a replacement for HMAC verification.

A webhook is a notification system modelled on the 'leave a note' pattern. Instead of repeatedly calling someone to ask 'Has anything happened?', you give them your phone number (the endpoint URL) and say 'Call me when something happens.' They call you with the details (the POST payload). You quickly say 'Got it!' (200 OK) and hang up to deal with it in your own time (async processing).

The HMAC signature is like a wax seal on the message: only someone with the secret key can create a valid seal, and anyone with the key can verify it. If the seal is broken or forged, you reject the message without opening it.

Idempotency is the 'what if they call twice?' scenario. If the phone company accidentally delivers the same notification twice (retries), your system handles it gracefully — by checking whether you already acted on this specific notification (by ID) before processing it again.
A webhook is an HTTP callback — the provider POSTs event payloads to the consumer's registered URL; the consumer acknowledges within a timeout.
Providers implement at-least-once delivery with exponential backoff retries — consumers must implement idempotency using event IDs.
Always verify HMAC-SHA256 signatures using constant-time comparison before trusting any webhook payload.
Return 200 immediately and process asynchronously — slow synchronous processing causes timeouts and duplicate delivery.
Implement event ID deduplication with a 24-hour TTL Redis cache to handle retries without side effects.
Protect against replay attacks by validating that the event timestamp is within an acceptable window (typically 5 minutes).
Use ngrok, Cloudflare Tunnel, or provider-specific CLIs for local webhook development and testing.
For high-volume event streams, use a lightweight receiver → message bus → worker architecture to decouple ingestion from processing.
